topic.topic: How should we measure agent process fidelity?

topic_summary: co_scientist research package for: How should we measure agent process fidelity?

primitive_definitions.name: Process fidelity
primitive_definitions.definition: The degree to which an agent's action sequence conforms to a prescribed canonical process in terms of both step identity and step order/frequency.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is the target property being measured, and it decomposes into step-level and distributional aspects that cannot be easily reduced further.

primitive_definitions.name: Jensen-Shannon divergence (JSD)
primitive_definitions.definition: A symmetric and finite information-theoretic measure of dissimilarity between two probability distributions, defined as the average of KL-divergences to the mixture distribution.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: JSD is used as the core metric to quantify distance between observed and expected step distributions; it is bounded and symmetric, making it suitable for comparison.

primitive_definitions.name: Step distribution
primitive_definitions.definition: A normalized histogram of the frequencies of each distinct step type observed across multiple runs of an agent on a given task.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: The distribution captures both which steps are executed and how often, enabling statistical comparison beyond discrete step matching.

assumptions.assumption: Each procedural task has a well-defined set of canonical steps with known expected frequencies.
assumptions.rationale: Without a prescriptive step distribution, the JSD cannot be computed; the target distribution must be defined a priori.
assumptions.possible_failure: If tasks are highly open-ended or steps are not predetermined, the target distribution is undefined, making the metric inapplicable.

assumptions.assumption: Multiple executions (runs) of the same task by the same agent are independent and identically distributed with respect to step selection.
assumptions.rationale: To compute a reliable empirical step distribution, multiple runs must be performed; i.i.d. ensures the distribution is representative.
assumptions.possible_failure: If agents exhibit learning or fatigue across runs, the distribution may not be stationary, biasing the JSD estimate.

variables.name: topic condition
variables.description: The main condition or task distribution studied by the baseline.

variables.name: proposed mechanism
variables.description: A high-fidelity agent should produce step frequency distributions that match the target process distribution; the Jensen-Shannon divergence quantifies the discrepancy. Lower JSD indicates higher fidelity because the agent’s behavior is statistically closer to the ideal.

variables.name: research quality
variables.description: Whether the proposed question is falsifiable and experimentable.

causal_chain.step: A high-fidelity agent should produce step frequency distributions that match the target process distribution; the Jensen-Shannon divergence quantifies the discrepancy. Lower JSD indicates higher fidelity because the agent’s behavior is statistically closer to the ideal.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: A high-fidelity agent should produce step frequency distributions that match the target process distribution; the Jensen-Shannon divergence quantifies the discrepancy. Lower JSD indicates higher fidelity because the agent’s behavior is statistically closer to the ideal.

tensions.statement: Current metrics either focus on exact sequence matching (accuracy) or require expensive graph comparisons; this distributional approach balances detail with statistical tractability, but may miss subtle ordering deviations.
tensions.why_it_matters: This is the first application of Jensen-Shannon divergence to measure process fidelity in LLM agents, moving beyond exact match to capture statistical adherence. Prior work used sequence accuracy or exact ordering; this approach is robust to stochastic behavior and requires only multiple runs.

candidate_questions.question: Can Jensen-Shannon divergence between an agent's empirical step distribution and the canonical step distribution reliably measure agent process fidelity across various procedural tasks?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: This is the first application of Jensen-Shannon divergence to measure process fidelity in LLM agents, moving beyond exact match to capture statistical adherence. Prior work used sequence accuracy or exact ordering; this approach is robust to stochastic behavior and requires only multiple runs.

causal_chain.step: A high-fidelity agent should produce step frequency distributions that match the target process distribution; the Jensen-Shannon divergence quantifies the discrepancy. Lower JSD indicates higher fidelity because the agent’s behavior is statistically closer to the ideal.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: A high-fidelity agent should produce step frequency distributions that match the target process distribution; the Jensen-Shannon divergence quantifies the discrepancy. Lower JSD indicates higher fidelity because the agent’s behavior is statistically closer to the ideal.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: Current metrics either focus on exact sequence matching (accuracy) or require expensive graph comparisons; this distributional approach balances detail with statistical tractability, but may miss subtle ordering deviations.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: This is the first application of Jensen-Shannon divergence to measure process fidelity in LLM agents, moving beyond exact match to capture statistical adherence. Prior work used sequence accuracy or exact ordering; this approach is robust to stochastic behavior and requires only multiple runs.

research_question.question: Can Jensen-Shannon divergence between an agent's empirical step distribution and the canonical step distribution reliably measure agent process fidelity across various procedural tasks?
research_question.expected_contribution: This is the first application of Jensen-Shannon divergence to measure process fidelity in LLM agents, moving beyond exact match to capture statistical adherence. Prior work used sequence accuracy or exact ordering; this approach is robust to stochastic behavior and requires only multiple runs.

hypothesis.statement: Agent process fidelity is inversely proportional to the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the empirical distribution of agent's steps (normalized by frequency) and the target distribution defined by the ideal process, with lower divergence meaning higher fidelity.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Jensen-Shannon divergence will be negatively correlated with human-judged process fidelity scores (Spearman ρ < -0.5).

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Define 10 procedural tasks (e.g., form filling, file manipulation, question answering with tools) each with a canonical step sequence and expected step frequencies (some steps may repeat). For each task, collect 100 execution traces from 5 different LLM agents (e.g., GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Mistral). For each agent-task combination, compute the empirical step frequency histogram across 100 runs, then compute the JSD between that histogram and the ideal histogram (uniform or as specified). Obtain human fidelity ratings (1-5 scale) on a subset of 50 individual traces per agent-task (blinded, averaged). Average the JSD per agent. Compute Spearman correlation between average JSD and average human fidelity rating across agents and tasks.
controls: Task instructions and prompts (identical across agents)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If the Spearman correlation between JSD and human fidelity ratings is not significantly negative (p > 0.05 or ρ > -0.2), then the hypothesis that JSD measures process fidelity is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: JSD values are lower for agents known to follow instructions well (e.g., GPT-4) than for weaker agents (e.g., Llama)
JSD captures differences in step frequency even when step accuracy is perfect
rejects_hypothesis: JSD is insensitive to known fidelity differences

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If falsified, the distributional approach alone may be insufficient; we would then integrate step-order metrics (e.g., Levenshtein distance on step sequences) with JSD to create a composite fidelity score.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Each procedural task has a well-defined set of canonical steps with known expected frequencies.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What mechanism explains failure of: Can Jensen-Shannon divergence between an agent's empirical step distribution and the canonical step distribution reliably measure agent process fidelity across various procedural tasks?

hypotheses.question: Can structural graph metrics like graph edit distance effectively measure how closely an agent's action sequence matches a gold-standard process trace?
hypotheses.hypothesis: Agent process fidelity can be quantified by computing the normalized graph edit distance between the directed graph of agent actions (steps and transitions) and a canonical process graph, with lower distance indicating higher fidelity.
hypotheses.mechanism: Graph edit distance captures both the presence/absence of steps and the order of transitions, providing a fine-grained comparison of process structure beyond simple sequence accuracy.
hypotheses.experiment: Construct gold-standard process graphs for 20 multistep reasoning tasks (e.g., math word problems, tool-using tasks). Collect action traces from 5 LLM agents with varying instruction following. For each trace, convert to a directed graph and compute normalized GED to the gold graph. Compare GED scores against human fidelity ratings (1-5). Report Spearman correlation.
hypotheses.novelty_rationale: While sequence accuracy and F1 are common, graph edit distance has not been applied to agent process fidelity in LLMs, offering a more nuanced and robust measure.
risks: Graph construction may be ambiguous for agents with non-deterministic steps.
GED computation is NP-hard for large graphs; approximation needed.

hypotheses.question: Does causal intervention on the agent's process prompts reveal whether the agent's outputs are causally dependent on the prescribed process, and can this dependency serve as a fidelity metric?
hypotheses.hypothesis: Process fidelity can be measured by the causal effect of prompting the agent with a manipulated process description on the agent's subsequent actions, with higher consistency implying higher fidelity.
hypotheses.mechanism: If the agent faithfully follows a process, then systematically perturbing the process (e.g., omitting a step, reversing order) should cause predictable changes in the agent's behavior, quantified by causal effect size.
hypotheses.experiment: Design 10 base processes (e.g., for data analysis). For each, create 3 variants: (a) original, (b) omit step 2, (c) swap steps 1&2. For each variant, generate 50 prompts embedding the process instruction, then record agent actions. Compute the difference in action sequences (edit distance) between (a) and (b), and (a) and (c) across agents. Compare these differences to a reference human fidelity measure. High correlation indicates causal fidelity.
hypotheses.novelty_rationale: Current fidelity metrics are descriptive; this introduces a causal framework that directly tests the agent's adherence mechanism, not just output similarity.
risks: Causal inference requires large samples; variations may not be meaningful if process is not strictly followed.
Agents might ignore perturbations due to robustness.

hypotheses.question: Can information-theoretic divergence between the agent's observed step distribution and the expected step distribution from a process model serve as a measure of process fidelity?
hypotheses.hypothesis: Agent process fidelity is inversely proportional to the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the empirical distribution of agent's steps (normalized by frequency) and the target distribution defined by the ideal process, with lower divergence meaning higher fidelity.
hypotheses.mechanism: A high-fidelity agent should not only perform the correct steps but also exhibit a step frequency distribution matching the process's implicit probability (e.g., steps are equally likely if not specified otherwise). Divergence captures deviations in both step type and frequency.
hypotheses.experiment: Define 10 procedural tasks (e.g., form filling, file manipulation) where each step has an expected frequency (some steps may be repeated). Collect action logs from 5 LLM agents over 100 runs each. For each run, compute step frequency histogram and JSD to the ideal histogram (uniform or given ratios). Average JSD per agent. Compare to a composite fidelity score from human evaluation (weighted accuracy and order). Report correlation.
hypotheses.novelty_rationale: This is the first use of distributional divergence for process fidelity, moving beyond discrete match to capturing statistical properties of agent behavior.
risks: Step frequencies may not be meaningful if tasks are one-shot.
Requires multiple runs per task to estimate distribution.

critiques: H001 (Graph Edit Distance): High mechanism clarity and moderate feasibility, but limited novelty as graph edit distance is a standard structural measure; also computationally expensive for large graphs and ambiguous for non-deterministic steps. Less innovative compared to H002 and H003.

H002 (Causal Intervention): Highest novelty among the three by introducing a causal framework, and clear mechanism. However, feasibility is lower due to the need for large samples and careful causal inference, and risk that agents may ignore perturbations. Strong if resources permit.

H003 (Information Divergence): Novel application of distributional divergence to process fidelity, with clear mechanism and good falsifiability. Feasibility is moderate (requires multiple runs per task) and addresses statistical properties of behavior. Balances novelty and practicality well.
debate.synthesis: After pairwise comparison, H003 (information divergence via Jensen-Shannon divergence) emerges as the best hypothesis due to its strong novelty, clear mechanism, and good balance between theoretical appeal and experimental feasibility. It offers a distributional perspective that captures both step identity and frequency, which is missing in current fidelity metrics. While H002 is the most novel, its high resource requirements make it less practical; H001 is feasible but less innovative. H003 is recommended as the leading candidate for measuring agent process fidelity.
